findings.issue: User-spec contains substantial implementation detail that belongs in tech-spec: concrete class/method names (LogExportManager.composeExport(), CrashLogWriter.write(Throwable), LogcatReader.read(pid), DeviceInfoCollector.collect(), CrashState.shouldShowBanner()), exact Android API calls (Thread.setDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler, Process.killProcess(Process.myPid()), ActivityResultContracts.CreateDocument("text/plain"), ProcessBuilder), manifest attributes (android:process=":crash", android:exported="false", android:excludeFromRecents="true", android:taskAffinity=""), specific composables and icon identifiers (Icons.Outlined.ErrorOutline, Material 3 Card + Row { Icon, Text, TextButton, IconButton }), literal code strings (RuntimeException("test crash from About")), and the 'Как проверить / Агент проверяет' table which greps for exact symbol and file names (SanctumApplication.kt, setDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler). The user-spec should describe WHAT and WHY (user-visible behavior, constraints, acceptance criteria); HOW belongs in tech-spec.
findings.why_matters: Methodology requires a clear boundary: user-spec stays stable across implementation revisions, tech-spec captures the chosen implementation. Mixing them causes (a) user-spec churn every time an implementation detail is tuned, (b) harder review because the reader must separate product intent from implementation, (c) tech-spec becomes a copy-paste of user-spec and loses its role as the design-decision record. This violates the project's spec-driven pipeline pattern documented in the project-planning methodology.
findings.fix: Move all implementation-specific content to tech-spec and keep user-spec at the intent level: (1) Replace class/method names in acceptance criteria and in 'Тестирование' with behavior descriptions (e.g. 'a unit-testable component builds the .txt header in the documented order' instead of 'LogExportManager.composeExport()'). (2) Replace API-level specifics in AC with outcomes (e.g. 'diagnostic file is written to a user-chosen location via the system file picker' instead of 'ActivityResultContracts.CreateDocument("text/plain")'). (3) Move the entire 'Технические решения' section to tech-spec; keep only the high-level product constraints (single .txt, no share sheet, no telemetry) in the user-spec 'Ограничения' section. (4) Strip manifest-attribute enumeration from AC; keep the user-visible property ('crash reporter runs in an isolated process so that a crash inside the main process cannot cascade into it'). (5) Rewrite the 'Агент проверяет' table to reference acceptance-level checks (tests pass, debug APK builds, size budget respected) rather than grep-for-symbol checks.

findings.issue: The dedicated :crash process is a measurable step-up in complexity (separate Application.onCreate, separate Hilt graph, process-name guard, manifest split, bootstrap-loop concerns, dual-path logcat handling) introduced primarily to support US-A's 'instant crash screen' UX. US-B's restart-banner already covers every crash case (including the ones where US-A is supposed to fire) with a single-process implementation — the handler writes crash.log, the process dies, next launch shows the banner, user saves from AboutScreen.
findings.why_matters: For a test-phase hotfix with an audience of 'one close friend', the instant-report UX may not be worth the ongoing complexity (first isolated-process split in the codebase, dual code path for logcat, extra Activity, new Hilt-avoidance pattern). The same diagnostic outcome is achievable with only the restart-banner + AboutScreen button. Risks section already acknowledges native SIGSEGV bypasses :crash entirely — meaning :crash does not give stronger guarantees in the worst case.
findings.fix: Not a blocker — the interview explicitly locked this decision (Q3 'хочется сразу при краше если возможно' + Q4 'согласовано'). Recommendation: note in tech-spec an explicit revisit of the :crash-process choice if Phase 5 needs to simplify for public release, and document in Phase 5 backlog that US-A can be dropped without hurting US-B/US-C if the complexity is later deemed not worth it. No user-spec change required.

findings.issue: A few edge cases mentioned in the interview are missing from the user-spec acceptance criteria: (a) no free space on device when writing via SAF — interview said 'SAF сам покажет системную ошибку' but the AC does not state the expected app behavior (silent return? toast? same as IOException?); (b) crash.log exists but is 0 bytes (handler crashed mid-write) — banner behavior and .txt content semantics not specified; (c) filesDir/logs/ does not exist on a first-install AboutScreen export — no AC covers defensive mkdirs; (d) user double-taps 'Сохранить лог' before SAF opens — idempotency not asserted (Compose launcher is idempotent natively, but not called out).
findings.why_matters: These are low-probability but real scenarios a remote tester may hit. Without explicit AC, the implementer may resolve them ad-hoc and the tech-spec review may miss the decision. Size M feature with three user flows and 20+ AC should land each user-visible edge explicitly.
findings.fix: Add four short AC lines to the existing 'Критерии приёмки' sections: (1) 'If SAF write fails with any IOException (including no-space), behaviour is the same as the documented IOException path: Toast/Snackbar "Не удалось сохранить лог", crash.log on disk is NOT deleted, buttons remain active.' (2) 'If crash.log exists but is empty (0 bytes), banner still appears; exported .txt contains the header/device-info and marks the crash.log section as [empty].' (3) 'AboutScreen "Сохранить лог" works on a fresh install where filesDir/logs/ does not yet exist: empty sections marked [empty], header + device-info + logcat sections present.' (4) 'Tapping "Сохранить лог" twice in rapid succession does not corrupt the flow — only one SAF dialog opens.'

findings.issue: ACRA (Application Crash Reports for Android, Apache 2.0, mature, ~200 KB) is the established open-source library that does exactly this feature: uncaught-exception handler, isolated-process crash dialog, device info collection, file export, optional sender plugins. The user-spec does not acknowledge it. While the custom implementation is modest (~200 LOC), ACRA would cover bootstrap-loop safety, logcat capture, :crash-process setup, and dialog UX out of the box.
findings.why_matters: Project's 'open-source only' manifesto is compatible with ACRA's Apache 2.0 license. Rolling your own for a hotfix may mean maintaining edge-case code (process-name guard, logcat timeout, truncation caps) that ACRA has already battle-tested. At the same time, ACRA pulls in features we don't need (sender plugins, dialog with text-field, config builder) and adds a dependency to a codebase that deliberately minimises them.
findings.fix: Add one line to 'Технические решения' (or to tech-spec during TS drafting) explicitly acknowledging why ACRA was rejected: 'ACRA рассмотрена и отклонена — пришлось бы тащить всю библиотеку ради 200 LOC custom-кода, манифест Sanctum Machina требует минимизации зависимостей, и наш сценарий (один .txt + SAF, без sender plugins) не требует её функционала'. No behaviour change; just an explicit trade-off record so the question is not re-opened during review.

summary: Feasibility, sizing, and underengineering are solid — the feature is buildable on the current stack with no new dependencies, size M matches the scope, and the three user flows are well thought through with edge cases mostly covered. Main concern is a significant scope-leak into tech-spec territory (class names, API calls, manifest attributes embedded directly in user-spec). Approved — no critical findings — but the implementation-detail cleanup should happen before or during tech-spec drafting to keep the user-spec at the intent level.
